routing.prompt: Use $rust-code-structure to refactor these Rust structs, enums, free functions, methods, constructors, and repeated parameters around clear domain owners and invariants.
routing.reason: The prompt explicitly requests the skill's type and behavior-organization workflow.

routing.prompt: This Rust struct has a string kind, six optional fields, and boolean flags for mutually exclusive lifecycle states. Make invalid combinations unrepresentable without breaking the wire format.
routing.reason: Replacing Option-heavy pseudo-variants while preserving representation is a code-structure task.

routing.prompt: Review these repeated parameter groups and helpers that all operate on the same domain value. Decide which behavior needs a receiver and which stateless algorithms should remain free functions.
routing.reason: The request directly concerns domain grouping and contextual method versus free-function placement.

routing.prompt: The attached Rust API exposes public fields, a transparent type alias, several constructors, and persisted Serde data. Restructure it carefully and document compatibility effects.
routing.reason: The artifact supplies type-shape and compatibility signals owned by this skill.

routing.prompt: Keep all Rust types unchanged; decide how to split this package into a library, binaries, modules, and workspace members with appropriate integration-test placement.
routing.reason: Package, module, target, and test boundaries belong to rust-project-architecture.

routing.prompt: Edit the documentation and routing evals for an existing Agent Skill about Rust structs; do not change any Rust source code.
routing.reason: Maintaining an Agent Skill is repository authoring, not structuring Rust domain code.

behavior.prompt: Refactor an Option-heavy Rust lifecycle struct and its orbiting helpers into a clear model while preserving the serialized contract.
assertions: Inventories invariants, mutually exclusive states, transitions, ownership, call sites, existing impl blocks, and serialized representations before editing
Uses structs for values that coexist and data-carrying enums for closed mutually exclusive cases without splitting cohesive data mechanically
Places receiver-owned behavior in methods while retaining stateless, symmetric, adapter, callback, or module-level free functions when justified
Distinguishes transparent type aliases from invariant-enforcing newtypes and associated types
Updates callers and tests incrementally and verifies invalid states, transitions, exhaustive handling, and serialization compatibility
forbidden: Bans every free function, type alias, Option, boolean, or clone regardless of context
Changes public or persisted representation without an explicit migration and tests
Installs dependencies, publishes, or performs remote Git actions
Rewrites unrelated crates or modules to enforce personal style

behavior.prompt: Implement a repository-scoped Rust domain-model cleanup involving primitive soup, constructors, a god struct, state transitions, and public callers, then verify it.
assertions: Reads repository naming, API, ownership, error, serialization, and compatibility policy before choosing a model
Groups parameters and splits lifecycle data only where shared invariants, owners, or mutation rules justify it
Selects literals, constructors, From, TryFrom, parsing, or builders proportional to validity and construction complexity
Checks public fields, downstream construction, exhaustive matching, Serde behavior, auto traits, errors, and generated schema effects
Runs repository-derived formatting, linting, checking, and tests and inspects the final diff for churn and unrelated edits
forbidden: Introduces speculative typestate, traits, builders, or wrappers without demonstrated value
Makes invalid data easier to construct or hides fallible construction
Claims compatibility based only on compilation
Fabricates research, test results, or repository policy
